summary: DHH argues that AI coding assistants currently function like superb junior programmers — encyclopedic in syntax knowledge but prone to overly-complicated, subtly-defective solutions. He identifies the 'premise trap' as the core problem: the fundamental architectural assumptions in AI-generated code go unquestioned until too much time has been invested improving implementation details. He attributes this limitation to AI's training data being dominated by tutorials and simplified answers rather than proprietary codebases where real trade-offs are learned. He advises senior programmers to treat AI output with the same scrutiny they'd apply to junior code, and warns against letting AI sap programmers' own learning and stamina.
key_points: AI coding assistants exhibit the same 'premise trap' as junior programmers — their fundamental architectural assumptions are often wrong even when syntax is correct
Non-programmers using AI to build systems experience an initial rush followed by a one-step-forward-two-steps-back cycle when trying to make things production-ready
AI models are limited by their training data: tutorials, Stack Overflow answers, and content often made by 'the blind leading the blind'
The next big leap for AI coding likely requires exposure to proprietary, corporate codebases where real-world trade-offs are navigated
Senior programmers risk having AI sap their learning stamina and lure them into signing off on subpar work
money_quotes: The fundamental assumptions baked into the first draft of the code aren't questioned until you've already spent far too long improving the implementation.

AI at the moment is like a superb junior programmer. One with an encyclopedic knowledge about syntax and APIs, but also one saddled with the same propensities to produce overly-complicated, subtly-defective solutions.

It's an incredible rush to see a prototype come to life in mere minutes, but actually moving this forward to something that works reliably often turns into a one-step-forwards-two-steps-backwards dance.
The unfortunate reality that a fair chunk of internet programming content is made by the blind leading the blind.
Senior human programmers all got started on the same information diet, but eventually graduated to higher levels of understanding and mastery by working on proprietary code bases.
To verify the assumptions that have been baked into its path will require spending as much time to understand the choices as it would doing the work yourself.
Alert to the danger of luring more senior programmers, including myself, into signing off on slop, while it saps our stamina for continued learning.
key_insight: AI's coding weakness isn't syntax or knowledge but architectural judgment — it falls into the same 'premise trap' as junior programmers, building confidently on flawed assumptions that cost more to fix than doing the work yourself.
